era_context: Continuation of the Late Bronze Age collapse period. Egypt under Ramesses III faces mounting external and internal pressures. The Hittite Empire has already fallen; Mycenaean Greece is in the post-palatial period. The Levant is experiencing settlement reorganisation and the emergence of Iron Age cultures. In China, the Shang Dynasty approaches its end. The Eastern Mediterranean world order of the Bronze Age is definitively dissolving.

events.title: Ramesses III's reign — continued administration and military response
events.description: Ramesses III continues his reign, managing the Egyptian state through a period of economic strain and external pressure. Administrative records suggest increasing reliance on temple institutions and military garrisons to maintain control. The pharaoh's authority, while still substantial, is beginning to show signs of the fragmentation that will characterise the Third Intermediate Period.

sources.name: Papyrus Harris I and other administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Ramesses III's reign is well-documented; the specific year -1118 falls within it, but individual annual events are not always precisely datable.

events.title: Levantine settlement reorganisation — emergence of Iron Age cultures
events.description: In the aftermath of the Late Bronze Age collapse, Levantine settlement patterns shift dramatically. Coastal cities like Sidon and Tyre show signs of reorganisation and renewed activity, while inland centres are abandoned or depopulated. Archaeological evidence suggests the emergence of new cultural groups, including early Phoenician and Aramean populations. This period marks the transition from Bronze Age to Iron Age in the Levant and the beginning of the classical Levantine cultural landscape.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Levantine sites (Sidon, Tyre, Megiddo, etc.)
Historiographic consensus on Late Bronze Age to Iron Age transition

events.certainty_note: The transition is archaeologically visible but gradual; dating to specific years is approximate. The process spans roughly 1200–1000 BCE.
cross_references: Related to broader Late Bronze Age collapse

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the broader narrative of the Late Bronze Age collapse remains contested in terms of causation and regional variation. Some scholars emphasise climate change; others stress systemic collapse or invasion; most now favour a multi-causal model. The dating and cultural attribution of early Phoenician and Aramean emergence is also debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -1118 is sparse. Egyptian administrative records provide the most detailed information, but these are biased toward royal and temple narratives. Archaeological evidence from the Levant is fragmentary and subject to interpretation. Chinese sources remain primarily later compilations. The year -1118 is not individually attested in most surviving records; it is assigned by convention within broader dated periods.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Ramesses III's reign and administrative challenges.
Levantine settlement reorganisation is part of the broader Late Bronze Age collapse process.
